title: C++ Dynamic Memory
content:
### Dynamic Memory

Dynamic memory is allocated at runtime from the heap. It persists beyond the scope in which it was allocated until explicitly deallocated.

---

### Stack vs Heap

| Aspect | Stack | Heap |
|--------|-------|------|
| Allocation | Automatic | Manual (`new`/`delete`) |
| Size | Limited (typically 1-8 MB) | Large (system memory) |
| Speed | Fast | Slower |
| Lifetime | Scope-based | Until deallocated |
| Management | Compiler-managed | Programmer-managed |

---

### Allocating on the Heap

```cpp
int* p = new int;        // Allocate single int
*p = 42;

delete p;                // Deallocate

int* arr = new int[10];  // Allocate array of 10 ints
arr[0] = 1;

delete[] arr;            // Deallocate array
```

---

### Common Issues

| Issue | Description | Solution |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| Memory leak | Never deallocated | Use smart pointers |
| Dangling pointer | Use after delete | Set to nullptr after delete |
| Double delete | Delete twice | Use RAII |
| Buffer overflow | Writing beyond allocation | Use containers |

---

### RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization)

Tie resource lifetime to object lifetime:

```cpp
class Buffer {
  int* data;
public:
  Buffer(size_t size) : data(new int[size]) {}
  ~Buffer() { delete[] data; }
};
```

---

> **Key Point:** Every `new` must be matched with a `delete`, and every `new[]` with `delete[]`. Prefer smart pointers and STL containers to manual memory management.

quiz.question: What happens if you forget to delete dynamically allocated memory?
